steps.prompt: Which word is the main finite verb?

hints.facere: 'facere' is an infinitive meaning 'to do' — it complements 'videmur' but is not the main finite verb

hints.to see: 'to see' is the active 'videre' — 'videmur' is passive, meaning 'we seem'
hints.to conquer: 'to conquer' is 'vincere' — 'videmur' comes from 'videre' in passive voice

steps.prompt: What tense, mood, and voice is 'videmur'?

hints.Present active indicative: Active would be 'videmus' (we see) — 'videmur' with -mur ending is passive
hints.Perfect passive indicative: Perfect passive would be 'visi sumus' — 'videmur' is present passive
hints.Present passive subjunctive: Present passive subjunctive would be 'videamur' — 'videmur' is indicative

hints.vivo, vivere, vixi, victum: These are the parts of 'vivere' (to live)
hints.vinco, vincere, vici, victum: These are the parts of 'vincere' (to conquer)
hints.vito, vitare, vitavi, vitatum: These are the parts of 'vitare' (to avoid)

hints.Nominative: Nominative would be 'res publica' — 'rei publicae' is dative ('satisfacere' takes dative)
hints.Genitive: Genitive singular is also 'rei publicae', but here with 'satis facere' it is dative (to satisfy)
hints.Ablative: Ablative would be 're publica' — 'rei publicae' is dative here

hints.1st: 1st declension nouns end in -a — 'res, rei' is a classic 5th declension noun
hints.3rd: 3rd declension nouns have gen. in -is — 'res' has gen. 'rei' (5th declension)
hints.4th: 4th declension nouns have gen. in -us — 'res' has gen. 'rei' (5th)

hints.Genitive singular: Genitive singular is also 'viri', but here it agrees with 'nos' and 'fortes' as nominative plural
hints.Dative singular: Dative singular would be 'viro' — 'viri' is nominative plural
hints.Vocative plural: Vocative plural is also 'viri', but Cicero is speaking about 'us brave men,' not addressing them

steps.prompt: What is the tone of 'nos fortes viri'?
steps.target: Ironic — Cicero mocks the senate's inaction
choices: Ironic — Cicero mocks the senate's inaction

hints.Sincere praise of Roman bravery: Cicero is criticizing the senate for doing nothing about Catiline — 'fortes viri' is sarcastic
hints.A military rallying cry: This is not a call to arms but a critique of passivity — 'videmur' (we seem) undercuts any real bravery
hints.Humble self-deprecation: It is not humble — it is biting irony directed at the senate's failure to act

translation: But we, brave men, seem to do enough for the republic.
